title: Jammy dodgers
subtitle: Buttery shortbread rounds sandwiched with raspberry jam, with a window cut through the lid
excerpt: Sixteen jammy dodgers: a vanilla shortbread dough cut into rounds, baked pale, sandwiched with raspberry jam. The top biscuit has a smaller round cut from its centre before baking, revealing the jam underneath. The window is the whole point.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the shortbread biscuit section documents a creamed butter and sugar dough cut into rounds, baked pale, and filled with jam after cooling. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the cream biscuit chapter documents a similar enriched butter and flour dough for sandwich biscuits. The jammy dodger format , with a window cut through the lid to reveal the jam filling, is a British domestic baking convention documented from the Victorian period onward; the recipe here follows the public-domain shortbread method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked, unfilled biscuits in a bag. Fill after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baked, unfilled biscuits keep in an airtight tin for 5 days. Fill on the day of serving — jam softens the biscuit if left overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — shortbread colours fast, watch carefully

recipeTools.notes: A 6 cm round cutter for the bases, and a 2.5 cm cutter (or a bottle cap) for the window cut.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting a smaller shape from the centre of a biscuit lid before baking, so the filling is visible once the biscuit is assembled. The window is cut from the raw dough, not the baked biscuit — trying to cut a baked biscuit cracks it. The offcuts from window cutting are gathered and re-rolled with the rest of the scraps.

content.text: The jammy dodger is a straightforward shortbread sandwich biscuit. The base is a standard creamed butter and sugar dough, baked pale, filled with jam. What sets it apart is the

in the lid — a small round removed from the centre before baking, which reveals the jam below once sandwiched. Use a raspberry jam with a good set and enough sharpness to cut through the richness of the shortbread.

items.prepNote: good-quality, with a firm set — not a runny conserve

content.text: Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{icing-sugar}} icing sugar together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Add {{vanilla-extract}} and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add the {{plain-flour}} sifted flour and mix until the dough just comes together — stop as soon as it forms a cohesive dough, or it will toughen. Press into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan and line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the chilled dough on a lightly floured surface to 4 mm thick. Cut 32 rounds with a 6 cm cutter — 16 will be bases, 16 will be lids. From the 16 lid rounds, use a 2.5 cm cutter (or a bottle cap) to cut a window from the centre of each. Arrange all rounds and lids on the baking trays. Gather all offcuts, including the window circles, and re-roll.

Bake for 12–14 minutes until the biscuits are pale gold at the edges and set — do not let them brown, or the shortbread will taste bitter. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack and cool completely.

Spoon a heaped teaspoon of {{raspberry-jam}} raspberry jam onto each base biscuit and spread to within 5 mm of the edge. Press a lid on top, window-side up, so the jam is visible. Leave to set for 10 minutes before serving — the jam will hold the sandwich together once it has had a moment to settle.
